title: Trapped-Ion Multiqubit Gates are Compatible with Scalable Quantum Error Correction
authors: Ori Grossman, Yotam Kadish, Snir Gazit, Amit Ben-Kish, Roee Ozeri, Yotam Shapira
abstract: We construct a detailed microscopic noise model for multi-qubit (MQ) gate operations in the context of trapped ion architecture with all-to-all connectivity. We find that phonon heating and motional dephasing are well captured by effective single- and two-qubit error channels that can, in principle, act between arbitrary pairs of qubits. Nevertheless, the median magnitude of two-qubit errors between uncoupled qubits is substantially smaller than that of errors between gate-coupled qubits. Errors associated with photon scattering are shown to solely propagate to qubits participating in gate operations. Lastly, we combine all noise sources, assigned with experimentally relevant parameters, and explore the scalability of a quantum error correction (QEC) scheme based on the rotated surface code, as a function of error rates and code size. Our analysis bridges device-level physics and QEC performance for MQ gates in trapped-ion architectures.
summary: This paper develops a detailed noise model for multi-qubit gates in trapped-ion systems to determine their compatibility with scalable quantum error correction. It finds that despite the all-to-all connectivity, errors remain sufficiently localized to allow for a finite QEC threshold in the rotated surface code.
main_problem: Investigating whether multi-qubit (MQ) gate operations in trapped-ion architectures are compatible with scalable quantum error correction (QEC) by analyzing noise propagation and error rates.
main_result: The study demonstrates that MQ-gate-induced errors are compatible with a finite QEC threshold for the rotated surface code, as error propagation is largely localized to coupled qubits and certain error types are suppressed.
method: The authors use a Lindblad master equation formalism and Magnus expansion to derive a microscopic noise model, followed by quantum Monte-Carlo and Stim-based simulations of the rotated surface code.
model_or_system: A trapped-ion quantum computing platform with all-to-all connectivity, utilizing generalized multi-qubit Ising gates implemented via engineered spin-motion interactions in the Lamb-Dicke regime.
key_observables: Single-qubit and two-qubit error rates, bit-flip probabilities, entangling phase trajectories, and logical error rates as a function of code distance.
important_parameters: Gate duration (tau ~ 450 us), heating rate (50-100 Hz), dephasing rate (10-100 Hz), and code distances (d=5 to d=17).
main_assumptions: Assumes the Lamb-Dicke regime, neglects higher-order multi-qubit errors beyond two-qubit, and assumes Pauli twirling can treat coherent errors as stochastic Pauli channels.
figures_summary: Heatmaps of bit-flip probabilities, trajectories of entangling phases, comparison of analytical vs. numerical heating-induced errors, and performance plots of the surface code under different error models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MQ gate noise, develops a microscopic noise model using Lindblad dynamics, analyzes error propagation and locality, evaluates QEC performance using surface code simulations, and provides analytical derivations in the appendix.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous bridge between device-level physics (motional heating, photon scattering) and high-level QEC performance, which is crucial for designing scalable ion-trap architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The effect of Raman scattering on X stabilizers measurements of the distance 5 rotated surface code, implemented with a single MQ gate. (a) bit-flip probability (color, log scale) for each qubit, conditioned on a σz error on a specific qubit (here the ancilla qubit qerr = 29). Error rates associated with data qubits not directly coupled to the faulty qubit are markedly small, consistent with the connectivity structure imposed by the stabilizer. (b) The entanglement phase trajectories φn,qerr (t) as a function of time (dt = 0.01τ, τ ≈321 µs). Colors correspond to overall bit-flip probabilities in panel (a) for each n ̸= qerr. The entanglement phase evolution is compared to the MS...

FIG. 2. Heating and motional dephasing noise analysis. All values of Γh, Γs mentioned below are in-line with realistic hardware performances. (a) Bit-flip probabilities induced by heating during a MS gate (N = 6), for Γhnth = 50Hz and τ = 450 µs. We observe excellent agreement between the analytical solution and exact numerical simulations. Heating is dominated by single-qubit error with probability ∼Γhnthτ. (b) Bit-flip probabilities induced by motional dephasing for an MS gate (N = 6), with Γd = 10Hz and τ = 450 µs. Numerical results agree with the leading-order analytical solution (orange stars). Motional dephasing leads to both single- and two-qubit errors. Robust control techniques...

FIG. 3. Logical error rate as a function of the physical error rate, where all X (or Z) stabilizers are measured within a single MQ gate. Two-qubit motional-dephasing errors are introduced in two ways: In (a), a constant two-qubit error rate of p2q = 3×10−5 is assigned to every pair of qubits, while single-qubit error sources (including idle errors, heating, and scattering) are varied simultaneously with a common physical error rate pph. In this case, we observe a crossing at pc ≈2 × 10−2. The inset shows a magnified view of the crossing region. In (b), two-qubit errors act only between directly coupled qubits. Here, all noise sources, including two-qubit errors, are varied simultaneously...

FIG. 4. Studying QEC performance as a function of two- qubit error. (a) Gain factor (pph/pL) as a function of p2q, with all single-qubit error rates (including idle errors) fixed at p1q = 10−3 and code distance d = 5. We compare two mea- surement schemes: simultaneous measurement of all stabiliz- ers (blue) and a scheme that splits measurements into even and odd rows (orange). For p1q = 10−3, the splitting scheme yields higher gain. The relative advantage of the splitting protocol, Split gain

FIG. 5. Comparison between the probability distributions for the number of data qubits flipped due to hook errors, contrasting a sequence of discrete two-qubit gates with an MQ gate. Here, N = 10 data qubits are considered, together with an additional ancilla qubit that was traced out.
